customer-support.translation: передавать на более высокий уровень, эскалировать
examples: I'll escalate this issue to our technical team.
The ticket was escalated to tier 2 support.
customer-support.context: Used when a problem needs to be handled by someone more senior.

examples: Please create a support ticket for this issue.
I have 15 open tickets to handle today.
customer-support.context: A support request tracked in a ticketing system.

examples: The resolution time for this ticket was 4 hours.
We aim for first-call resolution.
customer-support.context: The act of solving a customer's problem.

examples: We must meet our SLA of 24-hour response time.
The SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime.
customer-support.context: Service Level Agreement — defines expected service standards.

examples: We triage incoming tickets by urgency.
The team triages issues every morning.
customer-support.context: Prioritizing support requests based on severity.

examples: Let me troubleshoot the issue for you.
The agent spent 30 minutes troubleshooting.
The support backlog is growing.
We need to clear the backlog before Monday.
We had an SLA breach on 3 tickets last week.
SLA breaches must be reported to management.
customer-support.context: When response or resolution time exceeds the agreed SLA.

examples: Our first-call resolution rate is 75%.
Improving first-call resolution reduces ticket volume.
customer-support.context: Resolving the customer's issue during the first interaction.

examples: Poor support leads to customer churn.
We reduced churn by improving response times.
customer-support.context: When customers stop using your product/service.

examples: Check the knowledge base before escalating.
We updated the knowledge base with new articles.
customer-support.context: A self-service repository of help articles and FAQs.

examples: Our CSAT score improved to 4.5/5.
CSAT is measured after each ticket is closed.
customer-support.context: Customer Satisfaction Score — a key support metric.

examples: Our NPS increased from 45 to 62.
NPS surveys are sent quarterly.
customer-support.context: Net Promoter Score — measures customer loyalty.

examples: Use the 'refund' macro for billing issues.
We have macros for the most common questions.
customer-support.context: Pre-written response templates in support systems.

examples: Knowledge base articles improve deflection rates.
Deflection reduces the number of incoming tickets.
customer-support.context: When customers solve issues themselves without contacting support.

examples: All stakeholders were invited to the review.
We need to manage stakeholder expectations.
account-management.context: Anyone with an interest in the account's success.

examples: The contract renewal is due next month.
We achieved 95% renewal rate this quarter.
account-management.context: Extending a customer's subscription or contract.

examples: We upsold the enterprise plan to 3 clients.
The account manager upsold premium support.
account-management.context: Selling additional features or higher-tier plans.

examples: We need to reduce churn in the SMB segment.
Customer churn costs us $50K monthly.
account-management.context: Loss of customers over a period.

examples: We conduct QBRs with all enterprise accounts.
The QBR presentation includes usage metrics.
account-management.context: Quarterly Business Review — regular check-in with clients.

examples: Expansion revenue grew by 30%.
We identify expansion opportunities during QBRs.
Our pipeline has 20 opportunities.
The pipeline value is $2M this quarter.
account-management.context: Collection of potential deals at various stages.

examples: Update the CRM after each call.
CRM data helps us track customer interactions.
Our ARR reached $5M this year.
ARR is the key metric for SaaS businesses.
account-management.context: Annual Recurring Revenue — total yearly subscription revenue.

examples: Customer retention is our top priority.
Retention improved by 10% this quarter.
Customer advocacy drives referrals.
We built an advocacy program for power users.
account-management.context: When customers actively promote your product.

examples: The onboarding process takes 2 weeks.
We improved onboarding completion by 40%.
account-management.context: Process of getting new customers started with the product.

examples: Let's define success criteria for this project.
Success criteria should be measurable.
account-management.context: Specific goals that define a successful outcome.

examples: Enterprise clients receive white-glove service.
White-glove onboarding includes a dedicated CSM.
account-management.context: Premium, personalized service for high-value accounts.

examples: The account health score dropped to 60%.
We monitor health scores weekly.
account-management.context: A metric indicating how well a customer is using the product.

examples: This sprint ends on Friday.
We plan our work in two-week sprints.
project-management.context: A fixed time period for completing set tasks in Agile.

examples: The product backlog has 50 items.
We groom the backlog every Wednesday.
project-management.context: Prioritized list of work to be done.

examples: Daily standup is at 10 AM.
Standups should last no more than 15 minutes.
project-management.context: Brief daily meeting to sync on progress and blockers.

examples: We hold a retrospective after each sprint.
The team discussed improvements in the retro.
project-management.context: Meeting to reflect on what went well and what to improve.

examples: Let's release an MVP and iterate.
The MVP includes only core features.
project-management.context: Minimum Viable Product — simplest version that delivers value.

examples: Our velocity is 40 story points per sprint.
Velocity helps predict future delivery.
project-management.context: Measure of work completed per sprint.

examples: This bug is a blocker for release.
Raise blockers immediately in standup.
We need to prevent scope creep.
Scope creep delayed the project by 2 weeks.
The Gantt chart shows the project timeline.
We use Gantt charts for planning dependencies.
project-management.context: Visual project schedule showing tasks over time.

examples: Task B has a dependency on Task A.
We need to map all dependencies.
project-management.context: A task that relies on another task being completed first.

examples: We created a WBS for the new project.
WBS helps estimate effort accurately.
project-management.context: Work Breakdown Structure — hierarchical task decomposition.

examples: Sprint planning is on Monday morning.
We select items from the backlog during planning.
project-management.context: Meeting to decide what work to do in the upcoming sprint.

examples: The burndown chart shows we're on track.
A flat burndown indicates stalled progress.
project-management.context: Chart showing remaining work over time in a sprint.

examples: Each story needs clear acceptance criteria.
The feature meets all acceptance criteria.
project-management.context: Conditions that must be met for a task to be considered done.

examples: Stakeholders approved the roadmap.
We need stakeholder buy-in for this change.
project-management.context: Anyone affected by or interested in the project.

examples: We automated the approval workflow.
The workflow includes 5 steps.
operations.context: A sequence of steps to complete a process.

examples: Automation saved us 10 hours per week.
We use automation for repetitive tasks.
operations.context: Using technology to perform tasks without manual intervention.

examples: We have SOPs for all critical processes.
The SOP was updated last month.
operations.context: Standard Operating Procedure — documented process steps.

examples: Increase throughput by 20%.
The throughput is limited by the bottleneck.
operations.context: The rate at which work is completed.

examples: The approval step is a bottleneck.
We identified bottlenecks in the process.
operations.context: A point where workflow slows down or stops.

examples: We need to ensure scalability of operations.
Scalability is a key design principle.
operations.context: The ability to handle increased workload.

examples: We must ensure compliance with regulations.
The compliance team reviews all processes.
operations.context: Adherence to laws, regulations, and standards.

examples: We conduct quarterly audits.
The audit found 3 issues to fix.
operations.context: Systematic review of processes or finances.

examples: We track 10 KPIs for operations.
KPIs are reviewed monthly.
operations.context: Key Performance Indicator — measurable value of performance.

examples: The ROI on this tool is 300%.
We calculate ROI before purchasing new software.
operations.context: Return On Investment — ratio of net profit to cost.

examples: Capacity planning ensures we have enough resources.
We do capacity planning quarterly.
operations.context: Determining resources needed to meet future demand.

examples: Process optimization reduced costs by 15%.
We focus on continuous process optimization.
operations.context: Improving processes for efficiency and effectiveness.

examples: Incident management follows a defined process.
We use incident management for production issues.
operations.context: Process for responding to and resolving incidents.

examples: We did a root cause analysis after the outage.
RCA helps prevent recurring issues.
operations.context: Identifying the fundamental cause of a problem.

examples: The runbook covers all standard procedures.
We updated the runbook with new steps.
operations.context: Documented procedures for routine operations tasks.

examples: Please keep your update brief.
A brief summary of the meeting.
The meeting agenda was shared in advance.
Let's stick to the agenda.
business-communication.context: List of topics to be discussed in a meeting.

examples: Who's taking minutes today?
The meeting minutes were shared via email.
business-communication.context: Written record of what was discussed in a meeting.

examples: We have 5 action items from the meeting.
Each action item has an owner and deadline.
business-communication.context: A task assigned during a meeting.

examples: We need alignment between teams.
Strategic alignment is crucial.
What are the deliverables for this project?
Each deliverable has a deadline.
business-communication.context: A tangible outcome or result of work.

examples: The scope of the project changed.
We need to define the scope clearly.
business-communication.context: The boundaries and extent of work to be done.

examples: The timeline is tight but achievable.
Let's review the project timeline.
business-communication.context: A schedule of events and deadlines.

examples: Regular touchpoints keep everyone aligned.
We have weekly touchpoints with the client.
business-communication.context: A scheduled interaction or check-in point.

examples: Let's sync on this tomorrow.
We have a daily sync at 10 AM.
business-communication.context: To align or coordinate on a topic.

examples: We need stakeholder buy-in for this change.
Getting buy-in from leadership is essential.
Here's a quick recap of the meeting.
Can you recap the main points?
Just a heads up — the deadline moved.
Giving you a heads up about the change.
Let me loop in the design team.
I'll loop you in on the conversation.
business-communication.context: To include someone in a discussion or email thread.

examples: I want to flag a potential risk.
Please flag any blockers immediately.
business-communication.context: To bring attention to something important.

examples: The subject line should be clear and specific.
A good subject line increases open rates.
email-writing.context: The title of an email that appears in the inbox.

examples: Use a professional salutation.
The salutation sets the tone of the email.
email-writing.context: The greeting at the beginning of an email.

examples: Choose an appropriate sign-off.
Common sign-offs include 'Best regards' and 'Sincerely'.
email-writing.context: The closing phrase before your name.

examples: CC the manager on this email.
I was CC'd on the thread.
email-writing.context: Carbon Copy — sending a copy to additional recipients.

examples: BCC the HR team for privacy.
Use BCC when sending to large groups.
email-writing.context: Blind Carbon Copy — hidden copy recipient.

examples: I'm following up on my previous email.
A follow-up was sent after 3 days.
email-writing.context: A subsequent message to get a response.

examples: Use bullet points for clarity.
The email had 5 bullet points summarizing key items.
email-writing.context: A list format for easy scanning.

examples: Please see the attachment for details.
The attachment is 2MB.
email-writing.context: A file included with an email.

examples: Check the recipient before sending.
The recipient replied within an hour.
Maintain a professional tone.
The tone of the email was too casual.
email-writing.context: The attitude or feeling conveyed in writing.

examples: Keep your emails concise.
A concise message is more effective.
FYI — the meeting has been moved to 3 PM.
Sending this FYI.
email-writing.context: For Your Information — no action needed.

examples: The approval is still pending.
We have 3 pending requests.
Please acknowledge receipt of this email.
He acknowledged the issue immediately.
email-writing.context: To confirm that something was received or understood.

examples: I'd like to reiterate the key points.
As reiterated in our previous meeting...
Our MRR grew by 15% this month.
MRR is the lifeblood of SaaS businesses.
saas-terminology.context: Monthly Recurring Revenue — predictable monthly income.

examples: ARR crossed the $10M milestone.
ARR = MRR × 12.
saas-terminology.context: Annual Recurring Revenue — yearly subscription income.

examples: Our CAC decreased by 20%.
CAC should be less than 1/3 of LTV.
saas-terminology.context: Customer Acquisition Cost — cost to acquire a new customer.

examples: LTV increased to $5,000.
A high LTV justifies higher CAC.
saas-terminology.context: Lifetime Value — total revenue from a customer over time.

examples: Our churn rate is 3% monthly.
Reducing churn rate is a top priority.
saas-terminology.context: Percentage of customers who cancel subscriptions.

examples: Onboarding takes 5 minutes.
We improved onboarding completion by 40%.
saas-terminology.context: Process of getting new users started with the product.

examples: Our freemium model drives adoption.
Freemium users convert at 5%.
saas-terminology.context: Business model with free and paid tiers.

examples: The API documentation is thorough.
We integrate via REST API.
saas-terminology.context: Application Programming Interface — how software communicates.

examples: Set up a webhook for notifications.
Webhooks trigger when events occur.
Our SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime.
SLA breaches trigger alerts.
saas-terminology.context: Service Level Agreement — commitment to service standards.

examples: We achieved 99.99% uptime.
Uptime is critical for SaaS reliability.
saas-terminology.context: Percentage of time a service is operational.

examples: Each tenant has isolated data.
Multi-tenant architecture reduces costs.
saas-terminology.context: A customer or organization using a SaaS platform.

examples: Automated provisioning saves time.
Provisioning takes 2 minutes.
saas-terminology.context: Setting up new accounts and resources.

examples: We offer Slack integration.
The integration is seamless.
Scalability is built into our architecture.
We test scalability with load tests.
saas-terminology.context: Ability to handle growth in users or data.

examples: We prefer asynchronous communication.
Asynchronous work respects time zones.
remote-work.context: Communication that doesn't require simultaneous participation.

examples: We span 5 time zones.
Time zone management is crucial for remote teams.
remote-work.context: A region observing the same standard time.

examples: Let's handle this async.
Async communication reduces meetings.
remote-work.context: Short for asynchronous — work happening at different times.

examples: Virtual standups are at 10 AM.
Keep standups under 15 minutes.
remote-work.context: Brief daily meeting to share progress and blockers.

examples: We're a distributed team across 4 countries.
Distributed teams need strong documentation.
remote-work.context: A team working from different locations.

examples: I work from my home office.
A good home office setup boosts productivity.
We offer flexible hours.
Flexible hours help with work-life balance.
remote-work.context: Freedom to choose when you work.

examples: Remote work can improve work-life balance.
Setting boundaries protects work-life balance.
remote-work.context: Maintaining a healthy separation between work and personal life.

examples: Many digital nomads work from Bali.
Remote work enables digital nomad lifestyles.
remote-work.context: A person who works remotely while traveling.

examples: Virtual meetings replace in-person ones.
We use Zoom for virtual meetings.
remote-work.context: A meeting conducted online via video or audio.

examples: Slack is our primary collaboration tool.
We evaluated several collaboration tools.
remote-work.context: Software that helps teams work together remotely.

examples: We're a remote-first company.
Remote-first policies attract global talent.
remote-work.context: A company designed primarily for remote work.

examples: We adopted a hybrid work model.
Hybrid work combines office and remote days.
We need at least 4 hours of timezone overlap.
Timezone overlap is essential for collaboration.
remote-work.context: Hours when all team members are available.

examples: We're an async-first team.
Async-first reduces unnecessary meetings.
